title: Spaces of Lorentzian Polynomials
speaker: Mario Kummer
abstract: We characterize the topology of the space of Lorentzian polynomials with a given support in terms of the local Dressian. We prove that this space can be compactified to a closed Euclidean ball whose dimension is the rank of the Tutte group. Finally, we show that a compactification proposed by Brändén is in general not homeomorphic to a manifold with boundary. This is part of a joint work with Matt Baker, June Huh and Oliver Lorscheid.

proof_stages.label: Stable and Lorentzian polynomial setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines stable homogeneous multi-affine polynomials, recalls that their supports are matroid bases, then introduces Lorentzian polynomials by matroidal/M-convex support and stability of all degree-two partial derivatives, noting that stability implies Lorentzianity by derivative stability.

proof_stages.label: Projective support spaces and comparison maps
proof_stages.summary: For a fixed rank-d matroid M, the speaker defines projective spaces of stable and Lorentzian polynomials with support M and compares them with the Grassmannian realization cell and local Dressian via Plücker absolute-squares and exponential valuation coordinates.

proof_stages.label: Logarithmic quotient and linear coefficient constraints
proof_stages.summary: After quotienting by positive rescalings of variables and taking logarithmic coordinates, the Lorentzian coefficient conditions imply binomial equalities that become linear and define an ambient vector space V_M containing the relevant logarithmic coefficient images.

proof_stages.label: Ball-with-Dressian-boundary theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that the projective Lorentzian space for M, in the logarithmic quotient V_M, is homeomorphic to a closed ball with precisely the boundary part coming from the local Dressian removed, giving in particular its dimension and a canonical ball compactification.

proof_stages.label: Euler-characteristic bridge and naive compactification question
proof_stages.summary: Using the ball compactification, the speaker relates compactly supported Euler characteristics of the local Dressian and the Lorentzian space by coning over the boundary intersection, then contrasts this logarithmic compactification with the naive closure in projective coefficient space and asks when such closures are balls.

proof_stages.label: Orbit-closure computation and Betsy Ross counterexample
proof_stages.summary: The naive-closure computation is reduced to variable-rescaling orbits indexed by faces of the matroid polytope, and for the rank-three Betsy Ross matroid on eleven elements a face-by-face computation of two stable boundary orbit closures gives Euler characteristic 70, disproving ballness for the corresponding naive compactification.

proof_stages.label: Elliptic matroid counterexample for Lorentzian spaces
proof_stages.summary: For the rank-three elliptic matroid E_11, defined by triples summing to zero modulo 11 and realized by torsion points on a plane elliptic curve, the reduced Dressian consists of five rays whose closures create a loop, yielding Euler characteristic 2 and showing that a Lorentzian polynomial space need not itself be a ball.

examples.name: Degree-six real zero-set flexibility
examples.note: In Q&A, a degree-six real polynomial whose nearby zero sets realize all topological types, after a linear change to a strictly Lorentzian polynomial, is used to argue that zero-set topology is not the right invariant here.
examples.engagement_reason: the degree-six zero-set example is invoked to say arbitrary topological types can occur after perturbation/change of coordinates, but no construction is run in the talk

passes.reason: The degree-six zero-set example is invoked to refute the idea that Lorentzian polynomials impose meaningful topological restrictions on real zero sets.
